function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result

You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---

results.content:
Based on the provided financial and operational disclosures, here is the breakdown of the Connectivity segment, its products and services, and how its revenue is categorized:

### **Segment Definition**
The **Connectivity segment** operates a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband network powered by thousands of Starlink satellites in Low-Earth Orbit (LEO). It provides global internet access—including to underserved rural and remote communities—and delivers connectivity to millions of consumer, enterprise, and government customers across 164 countries and markets. 

### **Products and Services Included**
The segment's offerings are divided into two main accounting categories: **Services** and **Products**.
*   **Services:**
    *   **Starlink Broadband:** High-speed internet services (median latency of ~25ms and fiber-like download speeds).
    *   **Starlink Mobile:** Satellite-to-mobile services enabling light data, text messaging (SMS), and over-the-top voice services (e.g., WhatsApp, FaceTime) via partnerships with roughly 30 Mobile Network Operators (MNOs).
    *   **Starshield:** A secure, specialized satellite network designed for government operations.
*   **Products (Hardware):**
    *   **Starlink Kits:** The physical hardware (inclusive of the satellite terminal) required to access the network. *Note: 100% of the "Products" revenue reported on the company's consolidated income statement is attributable to the Connectivity segment.*

### **How Starlink Revenue is Categorized**
Revenue is categorized by customer base and the specific performance obligations fulfilled. Because most customer contracts contain multiple performance obligations (e.g., the service + the hardware kit), the transaction price is allocated to each distinct obligation based on its standalone selling price.

**1. Consumer Revenue (Subscriptions & Hardware)**
*   **Subscriptions:** Driven primarily by monthly recurring subscription fees that vary by geographic market and download speed. Revenue is recognized ratably over the service period (contracts are generally month-to-month).
*   **Hardware:** Generated from the one-time sale of the Starlink Kit. Revenue is recognized upon delivery to the consumer.

**2. Enterprise Revenue**
*   **Service Structures:** Enterprise contracts (which include aviation, maritime, mobility, and other corporate businesses) are customized and structured as a combination of fixed subscriptions, variable data consumption, capacity agreements, or on a percentage-of-completion basis.
*   **Starlink Mobile (MNOs):** Revenue from Mobile Network Operators is categorized under Enterprise & Government. The company charges MNOs either a fixed fee or a per-mobile-user fee, which the carrier typically passes through to the end customer.
*   **Hardware:** For certain enterprise customers, Starlink Kit revenue is recognized upon *installation* rather than upon delivery.

**3. Government Revenue**
*   **Starshield Contracts:** Generated via long-term, multi-year government contracts. 
*   **Recognition:** Because these are long-term in nature, revenue is recognized over time using the **cost-to-cost input method** (based on costs like materials and labor hours incurred to date relative to total estimated costs).

### **Deferred Revenue & Accounting Treatment**
*   **Service Revenue Categorization:** Service revenues are classified as either *fixed-price* (requiring advance or recurring monthly payments) or *variable-priced* (based on actual data consumption).
*   **Deferred Revenue:** Cash received in advance for broadband and mobile connectivity services is recorded as deferred revenue on the balance sheet and recognized as revenue either ratably over the subscription term or as actual data is consumed. A significant portion of the company's total deferred revenue balance is tied to Connectivity enterprise and government contracts.

Based on the provided financial statements and preamble disclosures, here is how SpaceX defines its reportable segments, the specific revenue recognition policies for the Connectivity segment, and the key risk factors associated with its segment reporting.

### 1. Definition of Reportable Segments
Following corporate mergers and a realignment effective in Q1 2026, SpaceX’s Chief Executive Officer (acting as the Chief Operating Decision Maker, or CODM) organizes the company, allocates resources, and measures performance across **three operating and reportable segments**:

*   **Space:** Designs, manufactures, and launches reusable rockets to provide high-cadence, reliable, and affordable access to space at an unprecedented scale.
*   **Connectivity:** Operates a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband network powered by thousands of Starlink satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, delivering connectivity to consumer, enterprise, and government customers globally.
*   **AI:** Operates a vertically integrated AI platform that includes the frontier LLM "Grok," AI solutions for consumers and enterprises, the "X" platform (a real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform), and AI computational infrastructure.

### 2. Revenue Recognition Policies: Connectivity Segment
The Connectivity segment generates revenue through broadband, Starlink Mobile, Starshield, and hardware sales. The accounting policies are structured as follows:

*   **Multiple Performance Obligations:** Substantially all Starlink customer contracts contain multiple distinct performance obligations—typically (i) broadband/mobile services and (ii) the Starlink Kit (hardware/terminal). The transaction price is allocated to each distinct obligation based on its standalone selling price.
*   **Broadband and Mobile Services (Recognized *Over Time*):** Revenue is recognized over time as the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits.
    *   *Fixed-Price/Subscription:* Advance or recurring payments are recorded as deferred revenue and recognized ratably over the subscription term. Broadband contracts are generally month-to-month (revenue equals the amount billed that month). Mobile agreements are generally multi-year (1 to 5 years) but allow for customer termination at any time.
    *   *Variable-Price:* Recognized based on actual data consumption/usage.
*   **Starshield Contracts (Recognized *Over Time*):** Multi-year contracts for secure government and national security satellite networks are recognized over time using the **cost-to-cost input method**. Revenue is recorded based on costs incurred to date (e.g., materials, labor hours) relative to the total estimated cost at completion.
*   **Starlink Kits (Recognized at a *Point in Time*):** Hardware revenue is recognized upon delivery to the customer (or upon installation for certain enterprise customers). This revenue is reported net of sales returns and credits.
*   **Inter-Segment Eliminations:** When the Space segment launches Starlink satellites for the Connectivity segment, no inter-segment revenue is recognized. Instead, the launch costs are capitalized into *Property, plant, and equipment, net* on the consolidated balance sheet.

### 3. Key Risk Factors and Disclosures by Segment
The preamble highlights distinct operational, regulatory, and market risks tied to the company's segment structure:

*   **Space Segment Risks:** Growth relies heavily on increasing launch cadence and payload capacity via the successful scaling of *Starship*. Unforeseen technical challenges, design modifications, supply chain disruptions, or anomalies could delay Starship deployment, which would subsequently impede the deployment of next-generation satellites, satellite-to-mobile services, and in-orbit AI compute infrastructure.
*   **Connectivity Segment Risks:** 
    *   *Regulatory:* Operations depend on access to highly regulated radio frequency spectrum and authorizations from the FCC and international telecom regulators. Acquiring these licenses is complex and time-consuming.
    *   *Competitive:* The segment faces intense competition from terrestrial fixed/wireless networks (which may offer higher capacity or lower costs in dense urban areas) and other satellite providers.
*   **AI Segment Risks:** As a nascent, recently integrated segment, it faces severe execution and operational risks. It requires massive capital expenditures for compute infrastructure, power generation, and model training. It is also highly vulnerable to disruptive technological changes, evolving regulatory standards, and well-funded competitors.
*   **Consolidated / Macro Risks:** Across all segments, the company is exposed to adverse global macroeconomic/geopolitical conditions, intense competition for specialized engineering talent, cybersecurity threats, substantial indebtedness, and the continuous need for significant capital expenditures to maintain its technological edge.

arguments.final_result:
## Starlink vs. OneWeb LEO Revenue Comparison: Implied Revenue Multiple and Commercial Maturity Assessment

---

### 1. Revenue Data and Annualization

**SpaceX Connectivity Segment (Q1 2026)**
- Q1 2026 revenue (three months ended March 31, 2026): **$3,257 million**
- Annualized run-rate (×4): **$13,028 million**
- For context, full-year 2025 Connectivity revenue was $11,387 million, meaning the Q1 2026 run-rate represents a ~14.4% step-up from the FY2025 average quarterly rate of ~$2,847 million.
- Q1 2026 YoY growth: 31.6% (vs. Q1 2025 of $2,475 million)

**Eutelsat OneWeb LEO Segment (H1 FY2025–26)**
- H1 FY2025–26 revenue (six months ending December 31, 2025): **€110.5 million**
- Annualized run-rate (×2): **€221.0 million**
- YoY growth: 59.7% (approximately 60%)
- LEO now comprises ~20% of Eutelsat's total group revenue (€591.6 million in H1)

**Currency Conversion**
- EUR/USD rate as of March 1, 2026: **~1.176**
- OneWeb annualized in USD: €221.0 million × 1.176 = **$259.9 million**

---

### 2. Implied Revenue Multiple

$$\text{Revenue Multiple} = \frac{\text{Starlink Annualized (Q1 2026)}}{\text{OneWeb Annualized (H1 FY2025–26)}} = \frac{\$13{,}028 \text{ million}}{\$259.9 \text{ million}} \approx \mathbf{50.1\times}$$

**Starlink's Connectivity run-rate exceeds OneWeb's LEO run-rate by approximately 50×.**

Sensitivity to FX assumptions:
| EUR/USD Rate | OneWeb Annualized (USD) | Multiple |
|---|---|---|
| 1.15 | $254.2M | 51.3× |
| 1.176 | $259.9M | 50.1× |
| 1.20 | $265.2M | 49.1× |

The multiple is robustly ~50× across reasonable FX assumptions.

---

### 3. What This Reveals About Relative Commercial Maturity

The ~50× revenue gap reflects a profound disparity in commercial maturity between the two LEO broadband networks:

**a) Scale of Operations**
- **Starlink** operates ~9,600 satellites across 164 countries and markets, serving millions of consumer, enterprise, and government customers. Its Q1 2026 Connectivity revenue alone ($3.26 billion in a single quarter) exceeds the entire annual revenue of most standalone satellite operators.
- **OneWeb** operates a first-generation constellation of ~618 satellites and is still in early commercial ramp-up. Its H1 LEO revenue of €110.5 million reflects a network that is still building its customer base, with ~600 aero antenna installations completed (out of a 1,500-aircraft backlog) as of December 2025.

**b) Revenue Diversification**
- Starlink's Connectivity segment spans consumer broadband ($2,148M in Q1 2026), enterprise & government ($1,109M), maritime, aviation, mobile-to-handset (via ~30 MNO partnerships), and Starshield (secure government networks). This diversification across customer verticals and geographies is characteristic of a commercially mature platform.
- OneWeb's LEO revenue is reported as a single undifferentiated line item, suggesting a less diversified revenue base still concentrated in B2B/government and wholesale distribution partnerships rather than direct-to-consumer scale.

**c) Growth Trajectory**
- Both networks are growing rapidly: Starlink at ~32% YoY (Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025) and OneWeb at ~60% YoY. OneWeb's higher growth rate is characteristic of an earlier-stage network growing off a much smaller base. At €221 million annualized, OneWeb's LEO revenue is roughly where Starlink's was in approximately 2020–2021, before its exponential subscriber ramp.

**d) Ecosystem Maturity**
- Starlink has achieved vertical integration (satellite manufacturing, launch via Falcon 9/Starship, ground terminals, direct consumer sales), enabling rapid iteration and cost reduction. OneWeb relies on third-party launch providers and a distribution-partner model, which constrains deployment speed and margin capture.

---

### 4. Disclosure Differences Limiting Direct Comparison Precision

Several material disclosure differences between the SpaceX S-1 and Eutelsat's H1 FY2025–26 earnings report limit the precision of a direct comparison:

| Dimension | SpaceX S-1 | Eutelsat H1 Report |
|---|---|---|
| **Segment Scope** | Connectivity includes consumer broadband, enterprise, government (Starshield), mobile-to-handset, AND hardware (Starlink kits). 100% of "Products" revenue is attributed to Connectivity. | LEO is reported as a single line item. It is unclear whether terminal/hardware sales are included in the €110.5M figure or reported elsewhere. |
| **Fiscal Year Alignment** | Calendar-year basis (Q1 2026 = Jan–Mar 2026). | Fiscal year runs July–June (H1 FY2025–26 = Jul–Dec 2025). The two annualized figures are based on partially overlapping but non-identical time periods. |
| **Consolidation Scope** | Retrospectively recast under common-control accounting to include xAI (merged Feb 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged Mar 28, 2025) for all periods. The Connectivity segment sits within this recast consolidated entity. | OneWeb LEO is a sub-segment of Eutelsat's consolidated GEO-LEO operations. Inter-segment eliminations between GEO and LEO are not detailed. |
| **Revenue Recognition** | Detailed ASC 606 policies: hardware at point-in-time (delivery), subscriptions ratably over time, government contracts via cost-to-cost input method. Multiple performance obligations allocated by standalone selling price. | Eutelsat's earnings release does not provide comparable granularity on revenue recognition policies specific to the LEO segment. |
| **Sub-Segment Granularity** | Consumer ($2,148M) vs. Enterprise & Government ($1,109M) breakout within Connectivity. | No sub-segment breakout within LEO revenue. |
| **Inter-Segment Eliminations** | SpaceX eliminates inter-segment launch costs (Space segment launching Starlink satellites is capitalized, not recognized as revenue). This means the Connectivity segment's reported revenue does not reflect the full economic cost of satellite deployment. | Not disclosed whether Eutelsat eliminates inter-segment capacity sharing between GEO and LEO constellations. |
| **Currency** | USD | EUR (requiring FX conversion, introducing ~1–2% variability depending on rate selected) |
| **Seasonality** | Annualizing a single quarter (Q1) may not capture seasonal patterns in subscriber acquisition or enterprise contract timing. | Annualizing H1 (Jul–Dec) may similarly misrepresent full-year trends, particularly if H2 benefits from seasonal enterprise/government budget cycles. |
| **Backlog Disclosure** | The S-1 does not appear to disclose a comparable backlog figure for Connectivity. | Eutelsat discloses a ~€1 billion OneWeb backlog, providing forward visibility not available from SpaceX's filing. |

---

### 5. Conclusion

Starlink's Connectivity segment generates approximately **50× the annualized revenue** of Eutelsat's OneWeb LEO segment (~$13.0 billion vs. ~$260 million). This gap reflects Starlink's far greater commercial maturity: a 9,600-satellite constellation serving 164 markets with diversified consumer, enterprise, and government revenue streams, versus OneWeb's 618-satellite first-generation constellation still in early commercial ramp with a partner-dependent distribution model.

However, the precision of this comparison is limited by non-aligned fiscal periods, differing segment definitions (particularly the treatment of hardware revenue), the recast consolidation scope of SpaceX's S-1, and the absence of comparable sub-segment granularity in Eutelsat's disclosures. The ~50× figure should be interpreted as an order-of-magnitude indicator of relative commercial scale rather than a precise like-for-like benchmark.
